summary: Gruber analyzes the strategic dilemma facing Nokia's new CEO Stephen Elop, arguing that Nokia must settle on a single mobile software platform but has no good options. He diagnoses Nokia's fundamental problem as being a hardware-centric company that treats software as a subordinate component, citing insider accounts of hardware teams overriding software decisions. Gruber argues that adopting Android or Windows Phone 7 would reduce Nokia to a commodity OEM, while their in-house options (Symbian and MeeGo) are inadequate. The core argument is that Nokia's hardware-first culture is incompatible with a smartphone market where everything starts with the software platform.
key_points: Nokia's organizational structure gives hardware teams all decision-making power, treating software as a subordinate component that must conform to hardware timelines and constraints
Nokia's internal competitive analysis of the iPhone dismissed it with superficial arguments like developer annoyance at the App Store, showing deep institutional denial
Adopting Android or Windows Phone 7 would reduce Nokia from a platform peer of Apple to a commodity OEM alongside HTC and LG
Nokia needs a platform that makes people want a 'Nokia phone' rather than an 'Android phone,' but none of their options deliver that
Hiring a Microsoft executive signals awareness of the software problem, but the hardware-first culture persists throughout the organization
money_quotes: Bottom Line: Nokia is a hardware company that hates software.
It's not that hardware isn't important. But everything starts with the software platform.
Nokia needs a platform that makes people want to buy a 'Nokia phone', not an 'Android phone' or 'Windows phone'.
Going with Android or Windows Phone 7 reduces Nokia to the role of a commodity OEM, a peer not to Apple or RIM but rather merely to companies like HTC and LG.

It was not uncommon for us to give them code that ran perfectly by their own test, only to have them do things like reduce the available memory for the software to 25% the specified allocation, and then point the finger back at software when things failed in the field.
They even included 'developer annoyance at the App Store submission process' as a reason why the iPhone would ultimately fail.
MeeGo isn't ready, and has the whiff of vaporware.
key_insight: Nokia's existential crisis stems from a hardware-centric organizational culture that is structurally incapable of competing in a smartphone market where the software platform is the foundation of everything.
